verses.text: Crônicas abre com longas genealogias (caps. 1-9), fundamentando a identidade de Israel nas suas linhagens. Os filhos de Israel — os doze de Jacó — tornam-se a base tribal. Listas como estas podem parecer secas, mas trazem uma convicção silenciosa: cada pessoa pertence a uma história, e essa história remonta ao próprio cuidado de Deus.

A lista continua — Dã, José, Benjamim, Naftali, Gade e Aser. Doze nomes. Algumas dessas tribos se tornariam grandes, outras quase desapareceriam, mas todas são lembradas. A teologia mais profunda do Cronista está em sua contabilidade: Deus mantém registro, e nenhum nome escorrega da página.

Er e Onã , os dois primeiros filhos de Judá, foram destruídos por causa de seu pecado ( **Gn 38.6-10** ). Os descendentes de Selá são listados como um apêndice no final das genealogias de Judá ( **1Cr 4.21-23** ).

A principal linhagem ancestral de Davi descende de Perez, um dos filhos de Tamar, a nora viúva de Judá. • Hamul, um dos filhos de Perez, é completamente ignorado; o cronista pode não ter tido informações sobre ele.

A principal linhagem ancestral de Davi descende de Perez, um dos filhos de Tamar, a nora viúva de Judá. • Hamul, um dos filhos de Perez, é completamente ignorado; o cronista pode não ter tido informações sobre ele.
Para a genealogia de Zera, cp. **Js 7.1** , **17** ; **1Rs 4.31** (Maol pode ser outro nome para Zera).
separado para o Senhor: Veja a Nota de Tema “ Destruição completa ”.
Para a genealogia de Zera, cp. **Js 7.1** , **17** ; **1Rs 4.31** (Maol pode ser outro nome para Zera).

Calebe foi o descendente imediato mais notável de Hezrom , então o autor detalha seus descendentes ( **2.42-55** ). Este Calebe aparentemente não era o mesmo indivíduo que Calebe, filho de Jefoné, um descendente de Quenaz (veja nota de estudo em 4.13-15 ).

Os descendentes de Hezrom são apresentados até o tempo de Davi, em várias seções: a linhagem direta de Hezrom a Davi ( **2.9-17** ; cp. **Rt 4.18-22** ), os outros descendentes de Hezrom ( **1Cr 2.18-41** ), e depois os descendentes do filho de Hezrom, Calebe ( **2.42-55** ).\n\n

Os descendentes de Hezrom são apresentados até o tempo de Davi, em várias seções: a linhagem direta de Hezrom a Davi ( **2.9-17** ; cp. **Rt 4.18-22** ), os outros descendentes de Hezrom ( **1Cr 2.18-41** ), e depois os descendentes do filho de Hezrom, Calebe ( **2.42-55** ).\n\n

Os descendentes de Hezrom são apresentados até o tempo de Davi, em várias seções: a linhagem direta de Hezrom a Davi ( **2.9-17** ; cp. **Rt 4.18-22** ), os outros descendentes de Hezrom ( **1Cr 2.18-41** ), e depois os descendentes do filho de Hezrom, Calebe ( **2.42-55** ).\n\n

O cronista lista Davi como o sétimo filho de Jessé, enquanto Samuel o coloca como o oitavo ( **1Sm 16.6-13** ; **17.12** ). Samuel não fornece os nomes dos irmãos quarto, quinto ou sexto de Davi (Natanael , Radai , e Ozém). Mais tarde, o cronista lista um irmão chamado Eliú, que pode ou não ter sido um filho adicional (veja nota de estudo em 1Cr 27.18 ). O número sete (o número “perfeito”) talvez simbolize que Davi foi escolhido por Deus para governar.

O cronista lista Davi como o sétimo filho de Jessé, enquanto Samuel o coloca como o oitavo ( **1Sm 16.6-13** ; **17.12** ). Samuel não fornece os nomes dos irmãos quarto, quinto ou sexto de Davi (Natanael , Radai , e Ozém). Mais tarde, o cronista lista um irmão chamado Eliú, que pode ou não ter sido um filho adicional (veja nota de estudo em 1Cr 27.18 ). O número sete (o número “perfeito”) talvez simbolize que Davi foi escolhido por Deus para governar.

2:16 And Hur begat Uri, and Uri begat Bezaleel. Who yet is not thought to be the same Bezaleel we read of in Exo 31:2 though the fathers and grandfathers of both have the same names, and were of the same tribe. though the fathers and grandfathers of both have the same names, and were of the same tribe. 1 Chronicles 2:21 ch1 2:21 ch1 2:21 ch1 2:21And afterward Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead,....

2:17 Jether the Ishmaelite—(compare 2 Sam 17:25). In that passage he is called Ithra an Israelite; and there seems no reason why, in the early days of David, anyone should be specially distinguished as an Israelite. The presumption is in favor of the reading followed by the Septuagint, which calls him "Jetra the Jezreelite." The circumstance of his settling in another tribe, or of a woman marrying out of her own tribe, was sufficiently rare and singular to call for the statement that Abigail was married to a man of Jezreel.

2:18 POSTERITY OF CALEB. (1Ch. 2:18-55) Caleb the son of Hezron—The notices concerning this person appear confused in our version. In 1 Chr 2:19 he is said to be the father of Hur, whereas in 1 Chr 2:50 he is called "the son of Hur." The words in this latter passage have been transposed in the copying, and should be read thus, "Hur the son of Caleb." begat children of Azubah his wife, and of Jerioth—The former was his spouse, while Jerioth seems to have been a secondary wife, and the mother of the children whose names are here given.

2:19 And the sons of Jerahmeel, the firstborn of Hezron, were Ram the firstborn,.... So called by his father after the name of his brother, see 1 Chr 2:9. and Bunah, and Oren, and Ozem, and Ahijah; or "of Ahijah", as some (r) render it, this being the name of his wife, who bare him the sons before named, since mention is made of another wife in the next verse. (r) Junius, Tremellius, Piscator, Michaelis.
O Senhor escolheu Bezalel , um artesão descendente de Hezrom, para construir o Tabernáculo (veja **Êx 31.1-2** ).
Algumas das famílias de Gileade , listadas em outros lugares como membros da tribo de Manassés (cp. **Nm 32.39-42** ), também tinham uma conexão com a tribo de Judá.
Algumas das famílias de Gileade , listadas em outros lugares como membros da tribo de Manassés (cp. **Nm 32.39-42** ), também tinham uma conexão com a tribo de Judá.
Algumas das famílias de Gileade , listadas em outros lugares como membros da tribo de Manassés (cp. **Nm 32.39-42** ), também tinham uma conexão com a tribo de Judá.
Tecoa era uma pequena vila judaica a sudeste de Belém (veja também **4.5-8** ).

2:25 And the sons of Appaim; Ishi. And the sons of Ishi: Sheshan,.... Though they had each of them but one son, yet the plural number is used, their posterity being included, as in 1 Chr 2:8 and so in the next clause: and the children of Sheshan; Ahlai; who, from 1 Chr 2:34 appears to be a daughter.
2:26 And the sons of Jada the brother of Shammai,.... 1 Chr 2:28, Jether and Jonathan: and Jether died without children; the posterity of Jonathan are given the next verse.

2:27 And the sons of Jonathan; Peleth and Zaza,.... Of whom no mention is made elsewhere: these were the sons of Jerahmeel; not only his immediate sons, but their posterity, called from him Jerahmeelites, 1 Sam 27:10.

2:28 Now Sheshan had no sons, but daughters,.... And but one of that sort, whose name was Ahlai, 1 Chr 2:31 the plural being put here for the singular; or, if that is the name of a son, as some think, he died in his father's lifetime, and left no issue; so that there only remained daughters, and it seems but one by the next verse: and Sheshan had a servant, an Egyptian, whose name was Jarha; one born in his house, and brought up by him, and a proselyte, such an one as Eliezer in Abraham's family.

2:29 And Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant to wife,.... Having first given him his freedom, as the Targum premises; this daughter seems to be Ahlai, 1 Chr 2:31 which receives confirmation from Zabad, one of the descendants of this man, 1 Chr 2:36, being said to be the son of Ahlai, 1 Chr 11:41, that is, great-grandson: and she bare him Attai; the genealogy of whose descendants is given to the end of 1 Chr 2:41, of whom no mention is made elsewhere, but of Zabad, as before observed;

2:30 Now the sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel,.... Called Chelubai, 1 Chr 2:9 and is the same Caleb spoken of in 1 Chr 2:18 and his sons next reckoned were by a third wife, Azubah, Ephrath being dead, 1 Chr 2:19 and these sons were Mesha his firstborn, which was the father of Ziph; who gave name to the city of Ziph; there were two of this name in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:24 or this is the title of Mesha, governor of the city of Ziph;

2:31 And the sons of Hebron: Korah, and Tappuah, and Rekem, and Shema. One of these, Tappuah, is the name of a city in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:34 and there is also Bethtappuah in the same tribe, 1 Chr 2:53 which one, or both, might have their name from this man; and Shema also, 1 Chr 2:26. . 1 Chronicles 2:44 ch1 2:44 ch1 2:44 ch1 2:44And Shema begat Raham, the father of Jorkoam,.... Which Hillerus (s) takes to be the name of a city in the tribe of Judah;

2:32 And the son of Shammai was Maon,.... Who gave name to a city in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:55 see 1 Sam 23:24. and Maon was the father of Bethzur; prince of a very strong fortified city of this name in the same tribe, Jos 15:58, unless this was a son of Maon's, from whom the city had its name.

2:33 And Ephah Caleb's concubine bare Haran, and Moza, and Gazez,.... An half-wife, or secondary wife; for though this man seems not to have had more wives than one at a time, yet he had concubines with them; we read of another after this, if not a third: and Haran begat Gazez; whom he so named after his brother.

2:34 Sheshan had no sons, but daughters—either he had no sons alive at his death, or his family consisted wholly of daughters, of whom Ahlai (1 Chr 2:31) was one, she being specially mentioned on account of the domestic relations about to be noted.

2:35 Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant to wife—The adoption and marriage of a foreign slave in the family where he is serving, is far from being a rare or extraordinary occurrence in Eastern countries. It is thought, however, by some to have been a connection not sanctioned by the law of Moses [MICHAELIS]. But this is not a well-founded objection, as the history of the Jews furnishes not a few examples of foreign proselytes in the same manner obtaining an inheritance in Israel;

2:36 Attai begat Nathan, and Nathan begat Zabad: One link of the Jerahmeelite line through Sheshan (2:34-35). The genealogy preserves the line of an Egyptian servant adopted to perpetuate Sheshan's name when sons failed — an early biblical case of inheritance by adoption (cp. Eph 1:5).

2:37 Zabad begat Ephlal, and Ephlal begat Obed: The Jerahmeelite chain continues. *Obed* (servant) is a recurring covenant name in the Davidic line as well (cp. Ruth 4:21-22), though this Obed belongs to a separate branch of Judah's tribe.

2:38 Obed begat Jehu, and Jehu begat Azariah: Names common in later Israelite history attach to ordinary men in this register. Jehu here is not the king of Israel (2 Kgs 9–10) but a Judahite of obscure standing — a reminder that the Chronicler's roll honors faithful continuity, not famous achievement.

2:39 Azariah begat Helez, and Helez begat Eleasah: Six generations into the Jerahmeelite branch. The slow, generation-by-generation enumeration is its own kind of theology: covenant faithfulness measured not in single great events but in steady transmission across centuries.

2:40 Eleasah begat Sisamai, and Sisamai begat Shallum: Names of uncertain etymology, possibly preserving traces of the Egyptian ancestry mentioned in 2:34-35. The continuity of the line through cultural mixing testifies that covenant identity could absorb without dissolving (cp. Ruth 4:13-17).

2:41 Shallum begat Jekamiah, and Jekamiah begat Elishama: The Jerahmeelite line closes with Elishama — possibly the official mentioned in 2 Kgs 25:25 or Jer 41:1. If so, the line bore service into the very end of the kingdom of Judah, finally meeting the exile that Chronicles writes after.

2:42 Now the sons of Caleb - This was not Caleb the son of Jephunneh, but Caleb the son of Hezron, 1 Chr 2:18, 1 Chr 2:50. But some think that Caleb the son of Hezron was the grandson of Caleb, son of Jephunneh; but this is probably fanciful. The father of Ziph - "The prince of the Ziphites." - T.

2:43 These were the sons of Caleb the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah,.... This is another Caleb, the grandson of Caleb the son of Hezron, called after his name; he was the son of Hur, the firstborn of his wife Ephratah, 1 Chr 2:19. Shobal the father of Kirjathjearim: of the inhabitants of that place; they sprung from him; or, as the Targum, he was prince of Kirjathjearim, a city in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:60.

2:44 Salma the father of Bethlehem,.... Or prince of Bethlehem, as the Targum; not the same as in 1 Chr 2:11 he was the son of Nahshon, this of the younger Caleb: Hareph, the father of Bethgader; prince of a place of that name called Gedor, 1 Chr 4:4, and where this man's name is Penuel; Gedor was in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:58.

2:45 And Shobal, the father of Kirjathjearim had sons,.... Which shows that Kirjathjearim is not the name of a man, or of any of Shobal's sons, who are next mentioned, but of a place of which he was prince: the first is Haroeh, who is called Reaiah, 1 Chr 4:2 a word of the same signification: and half of the Manahethites; which Kimchi takes to be the proper name of a man called Chatzihamanaheth, another son of Shobal's; but Jarchi interprets it of the name of a place or province called Manahath, 1 Chr 8:6 over half of which Haroeh was governor.

2:46 And the families of Kirjathjearim,.... That dwelt there, of which Shobal was prince, and who sprung from him, are as follow: the Ithrite, and the Puhites, and the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; who had their names from Jether, Putha, Shumath, and Mishra, descendants of Shobal: of them came the Zareathites, and the Eshtaulites; that is, from the Mishraites sprung the inhabitants of Zeroth and Eshtaol, places in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:33.

2:47 The sons of Salma,.... Another son of the younger Caleb, 1 Chr 2:50 whose sons were Bethlehem, the inhabitants of the place, at least many of them, of which he was prince, 1 Chr 2:51 and the Netophathite; the inhabitants of Netophah, a place in the tribe of Judah, mentioned along with Bethlehem, Neh 7:26 these sprung from Salma: Ataroth, the house of Joab; Ataroth seems to be the name of a place in the tribe of Judah, where the family of Joab lived, the inhabitants of which were the descendants of Salma: and half of the Manahethites;

2:48 And the families of the scribes which dwelt at Jabez,.... A city in Judah, the founder of which, perhaps, was Jabez, mentioned in 1 Chr 4:9 in which learned men dwelt: the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and Suchathites; who sprung from men whose names were Tira, Shimea, and Sucha; and if they were not the posterity of Salma, yet dwelt among his, and so are reckoned with them; perhaps the latter might have their name from dwelling in tents; the former clause may be rendered, "that dwelt with Jabez", who was their master, and they his scholars;

2:49 She bare also Shaaph the father of Madmannah: Caleb's concubine Maachah (2:48) produced a line tied to specific south-Judah towns — Madmannah, Machbenah, Gibea. Personal genealogy and town-founding overlap freely in early Israelite memory; an ancestor's name often denoted the place his clan settled.

2:50 These were the sons of Caleb the son of Hur: The Calebite line of Hur (not the Caleb son of Jephunneh of the spies; cp. Num 13:6). Shobal — father of Kiriath-jearim — places the family in the territory north of Hebron, including the town that long sheltered the ark (1 Sam 7:1).

2:51 Salma the father of Beth-lehem, Hareph the father of Beth-gader: Two more Calebite settlements. Bethlehem here is named for an ancestor who founded or fathered the town — the same Bethlehem that would receive Boaz, Ruth, David, and ultimately the Messiah (Mic 5:2; Matt 2:1).
2:52 Shobal - had sons - "Disciples and priests, to whom belonged the half of the oblations." - T.

2:53 The families of Kirjath-jearim - "These were the children of Moses, which Zipporah bare to him, viz., the Jethrites, the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; of these came the disciples of the prophets Zarah and Eshtaol." - T.

2:54 The sons of Salma - "The righteous Bethlehemites, who had a good name, as the Netophathites, who removed the guards which Jeroboam had placed in the way lest the people should carry the first-fruits to Jerusalem: for the sons of Salma carried baskets full of first-fruits privately to Jerusalem; and having cloven wood, they made ladders, and brought them to Jerusalem to be laid up in Beth-mokad for oblations. These came from the lineage of Joab the son of Zeruiah;

2:55 the families of the scribes—either civil or ecclesiastical officers of the Kenite origin, who are here classed with the tribe of Judah, not as being descended from it, but as dwelling within its territory, and in a measure incorporated with its people. Jabez—a place in Judah (1 Chr 4:9). Kenites that came of Hemath—who settled in Judah, and were thus distinguished from another division of the Kenite clan which dwelt in Manasseh (Jdg 4:11). Next: 1 Chronicles Chapter 3
